axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: every current of teaching is received as *someone's*

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: from which the triple Veda re-establishes itself

axioms.name: eternal
axioms.cite: what is eternal is not any authored text-body but the vyavastha, the seed-order

axioms.name: eternal
axioms.cite: when all traditions pass away (in the dissolution between world-ages, on the cosmology in play), a seed persists

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: Never does anyone accept a tradition to be without an author

claims.name: vivartate
claims.cite: hence the triple Veda stands established

rejected_readings.label: eternal_agama
rejected_readings.rendering: A teaching-tradition, once established, persists eternally and requires a constant sustaining agent.
rejected_readings.why: Confuses the temporal manifestation (agama, which the verse says perish) with the eternal essence (the seed). Agama is established in each age by a kartṛ but doesn't persist eternally.

rejected_readings.label: rsi_composers
rejected_readings.rendering: The wise seers (rṣis) compose the Veda anew in each age according to their insights.
rejected_readings.why: Misrepresents the role of rṣis as creators/composers rather than perceivers. The verse says rṣis 'see' the eternal Veda, meaning they perceive what already exists (the eternal seed-order), not invent new teachings.

rejected_readings.label: seed_needs_agent
rejected_readings.rendering: The eternal seed-order also requires an originating agent (Ishvara/God) to establish it.
rejected_readings.why: Incorrectly extends the need for a kartṛ (which applies to the temporal manifestation agama) to the eternal seed (bīja). The verse distinguishes: agama needs a kartṛ; the seed is self-persisting and eternal.

notes: The verse threads between two levels of reality: manifestation (agama, which is authored and temporal) and essence (veda/seed, which is eternal and self-establishing). The key distinction is that the eternal principle (bīja = vyavasthā) is not itself authored but re-establishes the Veda each cycle through the perception of rṣis (not composition). The verse resolves the apparent paradox (how can the Veda be both eternal and need an author?) by distinguishing essence from manifestation.
